title: Dispatcher: hold stdin open for piRuntime until agent_end
description: piRuntime's RPC line shape is correct, but pi v0.74.0 exits the instant stdin closes (mx-d9b3ad). The current dispatcher writes the stdin string and immediately ends it (src/provider/local/sandbox.ts writeStringStdin), so real pi runs produce only response+agent_start+turn_start+echoed-user message_start/end and exit 0 — no assistant content. Wire the stdin-hold contract: pipe pi's stdin from a controllable stream, write the RPC prompt line(s), then close stdin only after agent_end arrives on stdout (or process exit). Consider an optional AgentRuntime.shouldCloseStdinOnEvent(event) hook or a runtime-level holdStdin flag rather than special-casing 'pi' in dispatch.ts (parity with the codex special-case at dispatch.ts:106 acceptable as V1 if cleaner). Blocks burrow-56bb (end-to-end dispatcher test) since the test would otherwise observe truncated traces. See src/runtime/parsers/__golden__/README.md 'Critical dispatcher invariant' section.

title: pi runtime: supportsResume=true via --session-dir + Run.metadataJson.session_id
description:
V1 piRuntime ships with supportsResume:false (paired with --no-session) per pl-5198 alternative #4 — resume is deferred so we don't add a code path no caller uses.

Once a real pi run has produced a session, lift the V1 posture:

- Remove `--no-session` from PI_FORCED_ARGV (src/runtime/pi.ts) and add `--session-dir <workspace>/.pi/sessions` (or whatever pi v0.74+ uses to pin session-id placement). Read pi RPC docs to confirm flag name + dir layout.
- Set supportsResume:true on the runtime descriptor.
- Wire session-id propagation: pi surfaces it in the `agent_end` (and `get_state` reply) payload. The parser already preserves the full envelope in payload; the run-loop layer needs to recognize the shape and write Run.metadataJson.session_id (claude-code is the existing precedent — check src/runner/dispatch.ts / RunsRepo).
- Implement buildResumeCommand in piRuntime: pass the prior session id as the resume token via pi's RPC contract.
- Extend src/runtime/pi.test.ts + the dispatcher e2e test (src/runner/dispatcher.test.ts) to cover the resume code path. Add fixture(s) for `agent_end` with a populated session id.

Related: pl-5198 alternatives #4. mx-d9b3ad (pi stdin-close exit semantics — relevant when wiring resume since the next run is a fresh process). mx-5fee0d (pi auth precedence — ~/.pi is not bind-mounted, so session-dir lives inside the workspace).

title: pi runtime: mid-run steering via pi RPC steer/follow_up commands
description:
V1 piRuntime renders steering as a `[STEERING] (priority: P) <body>` prefix on the next prompt (parity with sapling/codex) per pl-5198 alternative #3. Pi's RPC actually exposes `steer` / `follow_up` commands that could deliver inbox messages mid-stream.

This is a cross-cutting overhaul, not pi-specific:

- Burrow's dispatcher (src/runner/dispatch.ts:114-148) writes the prompt blob then closes stdin — same as claude-code. Mid-stream stdin writes would require an inbox-pull loop and a per-runtime async stdin writer.
- Sapling and a future runtime would benefit from the same seam — design the AgentRuntime extension so it's not pi-shaped.
- Coordinate with burrow-5db3 (Dispatcher: hold stdin open for piRuntime until agent_end) — the stdin-hold work is a prerequisite, since you can't write follow-up commands to a stdin that's already been closed.
- New AgentRuntime hook (encodeSteeringMessage(message): SpawnPayload?) or a per-runtime mid-stream writer interface. Pi maps each inbox Message to a {\"type\":\"steer\", ...} or {\"type\":\"follow_up\", ...} line; claude-code and sapling map to their existing user-turn shapes; codex stays no-op (one-shot).
- Update SPEC §13 (Inbox & Steering) to document the mid-run path once landed.

Depends-on: burrow-5db3 (file via `sd dep add` after creation).

Related: pl-5198 alternatives #3, SPEC §13, mx-37c351 (inbox encoding pattern), mx-63b005 (steering message render format).

title: pi runtime: principled event-kind promotion (compaction, auto_retry) to first-class SPEC §14.1 kinds
description:
The V1 pi parser (src/runtime/parsers/pi.ts) collapses pi's wider RPC vocabulary into SPEC §14.1's existing kinds with the full envelope preserved in payload (per pl-5198 alternative #2 — premature widening commits SPEC to a vocabulary we may regret). Once we have production observability on pi runs, decide which envelopes deserve promotion to first-class kinds.

Specific candidates seen in fixtures (src/runtime/parsers/__golden__/):

- `compaction_start` / `compaction_end` — currently collapsed to `state_change`. Downstream UIs (warren dashboard, greenhouse retries) can't distinguish a context compaction from generic agent state changes. Candidate first-class kind: `compaction`.
- `auto_retry_start` / `auto_retry_end` — currently collapsed to `telemetry`. Distinguishes a model-side retry from general progress envelopes; useful for greenhouse retry policy.
- `queue_update` — telemetry today; possibly informative as `progress` if other runtimes grow comparable signals.

Process:

1. After we have ≥N production runs (or dogfood weeks), grep `events.payload->>'type'` for the candidate envelopes and confirm they actually appear with useful frequency.
2. Propose new kind(s) via a seed → RFC against SPEC §14.1. Promotion is additive, so it doesn't break consumers (mx-cb3d62 envelope contract).
3. Extend the kind union in src/core/types.ts, dashboard view-model (SPEC §26 / mx-c17cc2 etc.), and any consumer that switches on kind. The parser stops collapsing the promoted envelope and emits the new kind directly; payload stays the same.
4. Bump CHANGELOG with the new kind and any consumer-facing implications.

Related: pl-5198 alternatives #2, mx-cb3d62 (SPEC §14.1 envelope contract), mx-a25ff9 (current pi event-kind collapse map).

title: piRuntime: honor frontmatter.provider / frontmatter.model in buildSpawnCommand
description:
Today `buildSpawnCommand` in src/runtime/pi.ts:149-154 builds argv as `[...PI_FORCED_ARGV, '--model', PI_DEFAULT_MODEL]` — both `--provider anthropic` (PI_FORCED_ARGV:118-119) and `--model claude-haiku-4-5` (PI_DEFAULT_MODEL:93) are hard-pinned. `ctx.frontmatter` is never read.

Warren landed the multi-provider surface (warren-f8c0) and per-project defaults (warren-618b) — operator overrides + project defaults + agent frontmatter all funnel through `runs.rendered_agent_json.frontmatter.{provider,model}`. But the wire stops at burrow: piRuntime ignores the frontmatter and pi always runs anthropic + haiku-4-5 regardless of what warren passes.

Symptom: a user setting `defaultModel: 'claude-opus-4-7'` in .warren/defaults.json sees opus in the UI helper text and stored in the run row, but pi runs haiku-4-5.

Wiring needed:
- Read `ctx.frontmatter.provider` / `ctx.frontmatter.model` (string-typed, optional) in `buildSpawnCommand` and `buildResumeCommand`.
- When provider is set and non-empty, substitute it into the argv slot currently pinned to 'anthropic'. When unset, fall back to 'anthropic'.
- When model is set and non-empty, substitute it for PI_DEFAULT_MODEL. When unset, fall back to PI_DEFAULT_MODEL.
- envPassthrough is a separate concern (mx-d46d5d already lets projects opt non-anthropic keys in via burrow.toml [env]) — this seed is just about argv flow.

Test: spawn fixture with frontmatter.provider='openai', frontmatter.model='gpt-4o' produces argv with '--provider openai --model gpt-4o'. Empty/whitespace frontmatter falls back to today's behavior.

title: writeStringStdin doesn't flush when holdStdin=true → pi never receives prompt
description:
**Root cause definitively isolated** (replaces all prior hypotheses on this seed — --offline didn't help; HOME=/workspace didn't help).

`src/provider/local/sandbox.ts:136-146` writeStringStdin:

```typescript
async function writeStringStdin(proc, stdin, holdStdin) {
    if (typeof stdin !== "string") return;
    const sink = proc.stdin;
    if (!sink || typeof sink === "number") return;
    sink.write(new TextEncoder().encode(stdin));
    if (!holdStdin) await sink.end();
}
```

When holdStdin=true (pi's path per burrow-5db3), sink.write() queues bytes in bun's userland write buffer but nothing flushes them to the kernel pipe. The else branch (sink.end()) is the only thing that implicitly flushes, and it's skipped. The prompt never reaches the child.

For claude-code (holdStdin=false), sink.end() flushes-and-closes, so it works.
For pi (holdStdin=true), the encoded prompt is buffered in bun and never delivered.

The fix is one line: add await sink.flush() after the write. Right below in the same file, makeWriteStdin (the mid-run steering writer) already calls await sink.flush() with a comment explaining exactly why ('guarantees the bytes reach the child before this helper returns'). writeStringStdin needs the same call.

**Regression introduced by burrow-5db3** (hold-stdin-open for pi). Before that change holdStdin was effectively always false and sink.end() flushed everything. After, the holdStdin=true initial-prompt path was never flushed.

**Conclusive evidence collected on live hung run_xzd93aejgg4z (burrow 0.2.10):**
```
$ ss -xnpe | grep '1871904\|1871905'
u_str ESTAB Recv-Q=0 Send-Q=0 * 1871904 * 1871905  users:(("node",pid=150,fd=0)...) <--   # pi stdin
u_str ESTAB Recv-Q=0 Send-Q=0 * 1871905 * 1871904  users:(("bun",pid=23,fd=21))    -->   # burrow write side
```
Both queues = 0 bytes since spawn. Burrow has the write fd open and connected, but no bytes have ever been written to the kernel pipe.

`lsof -p 150 | grep eventpoll` confirms pi's epoll fd 6 IS watching fd 0 ([eventpoll:0,8,10,12,15,16]) — pi will wake the instant burrow flushes. The dispatch path / runtime / argv / env are all correct; only the flush is missing.

**Acceptance test to add:** in src/provider/local/sandbox.test.ts, assert that after writeStringStdin returns with holdStdin=true, the kernel-side recv queue on the child's stdin fd has the written bytes (or equivalently: the child can read them synchronously).

title: encodePiStdin omits trailing newline → pi sits on incomplete JSON line, never processes prompt
description:
**Second pi-hang root cause, surfaced after burrow-029d's flush fix.**

`src/runtime/pi.ts:229-234`:
```typescript
export function encodePiStdin(prompt: string, messages: Message[]): string {
    const lines: string[] = [];
    if (prompt.length > 0) lines.push(piPromptCommand(prompt));
    for (const m of messages) lines.push(piPromptCommandFromMessage(m));
    return lines.join("\n");
}
```

For the single-prompt case (every fresh run with no steering messages), this returns one JSON envelope **with no trailing newline**. Pi's RPC mode is line-delimited — it reads stdin into a buffer and waits for \\n to know a command is complete. Pi never gets the terminator, so the parsed command never fires.

`encodeSteeringMessage` two functions below DOES append \\n correctly:
```typescript
encodeSteeringMessage(message: Message): { stdin: string } {
    return { stdin: `${piPromptCommandFromMessage(message)}\\n` };
},
```
The inconsistency is the bug.

**Conclusive evidence collected on live hung run_p2yj0mfjx2wk against burrow 0.2.11:**

After the burrow-029d flush fix:
- pi's stdin pipe Recv-Q=0 (bytes were drained into pi's userland buffer — flush worked)
- pi briefly spawned 10 libuv worker threads (some internal init happened) then settled back to 2 idle threads
- pi wrote /workspace/.pi/agent/auth.json (content: '{}') — pi got far enough into init to scaffold this
- No /workspace/.pi/sessions/<uuid>.jsonl created — pi never started a session
- pi has zero outbound TCP connections (never called anthropic)
- 5s of strace on the main thread: stuck in epoll_pwait2(6, ...) — main event loop idle
- 8s of strace across all threads with trace=write,sendto: zero syscalls captured
- burrow's events table for the run: zero rows
- pi's process tree: no children (no spawned subprocesses)

This pattern — pi has the bytes, pi initialized partially, pi never emits — exactly matches a parser waiting for line termination.

**Fix:** change `lines.join("\\n")` to either `lines.join("\\n") + "\\n"` or, more robustly, `lines.map(l => l + "\\n").join("")`. Mirror the convention encodeSteeringMessage already uses.

**Test:** in src/runtime/pi.test.ts, assert that encodePiStdin(prompt, []) ends with '\\n'. Same for the multi-message case.

**Why this slipped past burrow-029d:** the previous bug (missing flush) prevented bytes from reaching pi at all, so this newline bug was masked. The earlier 'eventually unstuck after ~2 min' behavior was probably the dispatcher closing stdin or writing a follow-up envelope, which delivered the missing terminator.

title: Workspace file listing endpoint: GET /burrows/:id/files (no path) or ?prefix=
description:
Follow-up to R-07 (plan pl-2467, closed 2026-05-09). The shipped surface covers single-file read/write — GET /burrows/:id/files?path=…&encoding=… returns one file or 404. There is no way to enumerate workspace contents over HTTP.

Warren's reap path needs this for the mulch_merge step (src/runs/reap.ts mergeMulch). Today mergeMulch shells fs.readdir(burrow.workspacePath/.mulch/expertise) to discover the per-domain JSONL files an agent recorded into, then fs.readFile each one. Filenames are agent-controlled — warren can't enumerate them up front — so a per-path GET loop isn't an option. Warren's R-07 adoption plan (warren pl-a31c, parent warren-0a83) migrated seed.ts + reap.ts seeds_close to HttpClient.files.read but explicitly deferred mulch_merge on this missing endpoint. Until it lands, reap stays half-on-disk and the shared-filesystem seam violation isn't fully closed.

Proposed shape (pick one):

A. GET /burrows/:id/files with no ?path= returns a recursive listing: { files: [{ path, mode, size }] }, with optional ?prefix=relative/dir to scope. Mirrors the prefix shape from existing buckets-style APIs and lets warren list .mulch/expertise/ specifically.

B. GET /burrows/:id/files?path=relative/dir behaves like a directory read when path resolves to a directory inside the workspace (entries + types), and the file-read 404/200 shape stays for files. Reuses one route at the cost of overloading.

Recommend A — single-purpose, doesn't overload the file-read 404 semantics, easy to add a recursive flag later if a consumer needs deep walks. Either way: same path validation as the existing endpoints (workspace-paths.ts: no .., no absolute, no .git / .gitconfig.burrow, symlink-escape rejected), respect O_NOFOLLOW on the directory open, JSON envelope.

HttpClient surface: HttpClient.files.list({ id, prefix? }): Promise<{ files: Array<{ path: string; mode: number; size: number }> }>. Same error rehydration as files.read (NotFoundError, ValidationError).

Acceptance:
- GET /burrows/:id/files (no path) returns workspace files recursively as { files: [{ path, mode, size }] }; ?prefix=relative/dir scopes to that subtree.
- Path validation rejects .., absolute prefixes, reserved entries (.git, .gitconfig.burrow), and prefixes whose realpath escapes the workspace.
- Symlinks inside the workspace are listed but not followed (no escape, no recursion through them).
- HttpClient.files.list shipped on @os-eco/burrow with NotFoundError/ValidationError parity.
- OpenAPI spec.ts + golden test updated; routes.ts entry added.
- Warren's mulch_merge migration is a downstream consumer, tracked in warren tracker — burrow side doesn't gate on it.

Upstream consumer: warren-0a83 (warren pl-a31c step 4 / warren-7f7c). When this ships, the warren-side mulch_merge migration is a small mechanical change in src/runs/reap.ts mergeMulch.

title: [warren] Inbound networking policy + sidecar exec endpoint (R-19 preview environments)
description:
Cross-repo coordination seed filed under warren plan `pl-2c59` (R-19 — Per-run preview environments), root seed `warren-1bcb`, design-lock step `warren-94d8`. Upstream consumer is the warren-side step `warren-83dc`. Full design contract is locked in warren `SPEC.md` §11.L (2026-05-14).

## Problem

Warren's R-19 ships per-run preview environments: each successful run optionally exposes `https://run-<id>.<warren-host>` that proxies into the burrow workspace's listening port, so reviewers see a working app instead of just a diff. Two burrow capabilities are missing today:

1. **Inbound networking.** Burrow's bwrap profile uses `--unshare-all` (`SPEC.md` §8.1) and only flips `--share-net` for outbound network policy (`network: 'none' | 'restricted' | 'open'`, §8.3). There is no concept of binding a host-side loopback port to a port inside the sandbox's network namespace. Warren needs to reach `127.0.0.1:<host-port>` and have traffic surface at `127.0.0.1:<sandbox-port>` inside the burrow — same architectural seam as the `.gitconfig.burrow` ro-bind (warren §11.G) and the per-run secret mount discussed under warren R-15: a controlled, declared, per-burrow channel that warren orchestrates and burrow enforces.

2. **Sidecar exec endpoint.** Warren needs to spawn `preview.command` (e.g. `bun run dev`) as a long-lived process inside the same workspace the agent used, *after* the agent's run reaches terminal success. Today `POST /burrows/:id/runs` is shaped around a single agent run with a streaming event surface; a sidecar is structurally different (no agent envelope, no `stop_reason`, long-lived, terminates on TTL/teardown/eviction from warren). Warren reap (`src/runs/reap.ts`) needs a contract that's distinct from `runs` so the same workspace can host both the agent run (terminated) and the preview sidecar (live).

## Proposed shape

### A. Inbound networking — declared per-burrow loopback port-forward

Extend `SandboxProfile` (`SPEC.md` §8.3) with an inbound declaration:

```ts
inboundPortForwards?: Array<{
  hostPort: number;     // host-side loopback port (127.0.0.1:hostPort)
  sandboxPort: number;  // port the process inside the sandbox binds to
}>;
```

Warren allocates `hostPort` (SQLite-backed, restart-safe; range `30000-31000` default per warren §11.L) and passes the pair when launching the sidecar. Burrow implements the forward:

- **Linux (bwrap).** A small per-burrow socat / userspace proxy that listens on `127.0.0.1:hostPort` on the host, forwards into the burrow's network namespace via `nsenter --net=/proc/<pid>/ns/net` (or socat's `EXEC` with the right namespace handle). Reuses the same userspace-proxy pattern §8.1 already uses for outbound DNS in `restricted` mode.
- **macOS (sandbox-exec).** Seatbelt doesn't isolate the network namespace the same way; the forward is a no-op (process already binds to host loopback). Warren's acceptance scenario 20 documents the macOS path as a skip per warren `mx-1d31f0`.

The forward lifetime is tied to the burrow lifecycle (released when the burrow is deleted) and is also tearable on demand so warren's eviction worker (warren plan step 7) can release the host port when a preview goes idle without destroying the burrow.

### B. Sidecar exec endpoint

New route, parallel to `POST /burrows/:id/runs` but for non-agent long-lived processes:

```
POST   /burrows/:id/sidecars
       Body: { command: string[], env?: Record<string,string>, cwd?: string,
               inboundPortForward?: { hostPort, sandboxPort }, readinessPath?: string }
       → { sidecar_id, host_port_bound: boolean, pid? }

GET    /burrows/:id/sidecars/:sid          # status: starting|live|exited|failed
GET    /burrows/:id/sidecars/:sid/logs     # tail of stdout/stderr (last N kb)
DELETE /burrows/:id/sidecars/:sid          # terminate; releases the forward
```

Process model: the sidecar inherits the same sandbox profile as the agent run (network policy, ro-binds, workspace bind) and runs **alongside** any active or finished agent run in the same workspace (warren §11.L: \"same sandbox, not a fork\"). Burrow does *not* gate sidecar creation on the agent run being terminal — warren controls the ordering (reap-time sub-step). Burrow does enforce a small per-burrow sidecar cap (recommend 4) so a misconfigured warren can't spawn unbounded sidecars.

`HttpClient` surface on `@os-eco/burrow`:

```ts
HttpClient.sidecars.create({ id, command, env?, inboundPortForward?, readinessPath? }):
  Promise<{ sidecar_id, host_port_bound, pid? }>
HttpClient.sidecars.get({ id, sidecar_id }): Promise<{ state, started_at, exit_code?, message? }>
HttpClient.sidecars.logs({ id, sidecar_id, tail_bytes? }): Promise<{ stdout, stderr }>
HttpClient.sidecars.delete({ id, sidecar_id }): Promise<void>
```

Same error rehydration as the existing `runs` and `files` surfaces (`NotFoundError`, `ValidationError`).

## Why this shape

- **Same isolation contract.** Sidecars reuse the existing sandbox profile rather than introducing a parallel \"unsandboxed sibling process\" mode that would violate burrow's isolation invariant.
- **Warren orchestrates, burrow enforces.** Port allocation, eviction policy, TTLs, LRU all live in warren (§11.L). Burrow's job is to make the per-burrow inbound forward declarative and to expose a sidecar lifecycle endpoint. Same division of labor as `network: restricted` (warren picks domains, burrow enforces) and per-run gitconfig (warren writes file, burrow ro-binds).
- **Bounds the blast radius.** The cap of 4 sidecars per burrow + the per-burrow port-forward (no host-wide port binding) means a runaway warren can't exhaust the host's port space or the kernel's network namespace count.

## Acceptance

- `SandboxProfile` accepts `inboundPortForwards`; bwrap profile builder generates the per-burrow userspace forward; sandbox-exec path documents the no-op skip.
- `POST /burrows/:id/sidecars` + `GET/DELETE` routes shipped; OpenAPI spec + golden test updated; `HttpClient.sidecars.*` shipped on `@os-eco/burrow` with `NotFoundError`/`ValidationError` parity.
- Per-burrow sidecar cap (default 4) enforced with a clear 429-or-409 error code (pick one, document).
- Sidecar lifecycle: process exit → state `exited` with exit code captured; explicit `DELETE` → state `torn-down` and forward released; burrow deletion → all sidecars terminated and forwards released.
- Inbound forward is testable end-to-end on Linux: a test sidecar binding `127.0.0.1:3000` inside the sandbox is reachable at `127.0.0.1:<hostPort>` on the host; bound to loopback only, not 0.0.0.0.
- A sandbox-exec (macOS) integration test asserts the no-op path returns `host_port_bound: true` without erroring (forward is implicit on macOS).
- Burrow's SPEC adds a section under §8 documenting the inbound-policy shape; ROADMAP entry references warren `pl-2c59` / `warren-1bcb` so the cross-repo coupling is discoverable.

## Coordination

- Upstream consumer: warren `warren-83dc` (step 2 of `pl-2c59`). Warren's downstream steps 3–5 (config schema, `runs` columns, port allocator) don't block on this; the reap-time launch sub-step (step 6, `warren-f156`) is the first warren step that does.
- Warren bumps `@os-eco/burrow-cli` in **both** `Dockerfile` and `package.json` + `bun.lock` once this lands (warren CLAUDE.md three-places rule, `mx-build` lockstep convention).
- Warren acceptance scenario 20 (`20-preview.ts`) exercises the full warren↔burrow path on Linux; macOS skip is documented.

title: pi runtime: widen envPassthrough to multi-provider key set (conditional by frontmatter.provider)
description:
## Symptom

Dispatching pi with `--provider openai --model gpt-4o` (or any non-anthropic provider) fails on first API call because `OPENAI_API_KEY` (and the other multi-provider keys) is never forwarded into the sandbox. Same for `GEMINI_API_KEY`, `GOOGLE_API_KEY`, `GROQ_API_KEY`, `MISTRAL_API_KEY`, `DEEPSEEK_API_KEY`.

The argv path (`buildPiArgv` at src/runtime/pi.ts:254) already substitutes the chosen provider/model correctly — only the env passthrough is anthropic-pinned.

## Root cause

`src/runtime/pi.ts:162` pins \`PI_ENV_PASSTHROUGH\` to anthropic-only:

\`\`\`ts
export const PI_ENV_PASSTHROUGH: readonly string[] = [
    \"ANTHROPIC_API_KEY\",
    \"ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN\",
    \"ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL\",
] as const;
\`\`\`

The comment block at :155-160 explicitly rejects forwarding `OPENAI_API_KEY` etc. on the assumption that argv pins `--provider anthropic`. That assumption no longer holds: warren's multi-provider surface (warren-f8c0, pl-4374) lets operators override the provider per run, and `buildPiArgv` already honors `frontmatter.provider`/`frontmatter.model`.

This contradicts the runtime contract recorded in mulch `mx-0db923` (warren-0e06 / burrow-8aff), which spec'd envPassthrough widening to include the multi-provider set at the runtime layer.

## Options

1. **Widen statically** — add the 6 keys to the readonly list. ~10-line diff + test update. Leaks host secrets into a sandbox that can't use them when running with the default anthropic provider.

2. **Conditional passthrough by frontmatter** (recommended) — extend `AgentRuntime.envPassthrough` from `readonly string[]` to `readonly string[] | ((ctx: SpawnContext) => readonly string[])`, and have `piRuntime` return only the key matching `frontmatter.provider` (`openai` → `OPENAI_API_KEY`, etc.) on top of the anthropic triple as default. Preserves least-privilege, satisfies the original comment's concern, lets each provider opt in implicitly through the dispatch contract. Non-pi runtimes (`claude-code`, `sapling`, `codex`) keep their static-array semantics — the type widening is additive.

3. **Doc-only** — tell operators to write `burrow.toml [env]` passthrough blocks per project. Zero code, but every operator hits the same papercut.

## Acceptance

- piRuntime dispatched with `frontmatter.provider = \"openai\"` and `OPENAI_API_KEY` set in the burrow process env sees `OPENAI_API_KEY` inside the sandbox.
- Same for gemini/google/groq/mistral/deepseek.
- ANTHROPIC_* keys remain forwarded when provider is anthropic (no regression).
- If option 2 is chosen: `AgentRuntime.envPassthrough` type widens to accept the function form; existing runtimes (claude-code, sapling, codex, declarative) compile unchanged with their `readonly string[]` literals; type widening is exported so warren's burrow-client facade picks it up on the next bump.
- Unit test covers the conditional shape: `provider=openai` → set contains `OPENAI_API_KEY`, missing the other provider keys.

## Cross-repo coordination

- Warren tracking seed: **warren-fe96** (warren repo). Warren-side wiring is already complete (pl-4374 closed: schema fields, override params, UI inputs, frontmatter forwarding, `buildPiArgv` provider/model substitution).
- Once this seed ships in a tagged burrow release, bump `@os-eco/burrow-cli` in warren (pin in package.json + bun.lock + Dockerfile per CLAUDE.md \"Relationship to burrow\"), extend acceptance scenario 16 (or add scenario 17) for a pi+openai end-to-end, then close warren-fe96 per the warren-0e06 / mx-f1703e pattern.

## References

- `src/runtime/pi.ts:155-166` — current PI_ENV_PASSTHROUGH + rationale comment
- `src/runtime/pi.ts:254` — buildPiArgv provider substitution (already correct)
- mulch `mx-0db923` — pi runtime contract (warren expectations on burrow piRuntime, including envPassthrough widening)
- warren `src/runs/spawn.ts:289-306` — provider override fold
- warren `src/registry/schema.ts:93` — withProviderOverrides

title: Wire resumeOfRunId seam end-to-end so dispatchRun can resume a prior session
description: Resume machinery exists (piRuntime.buildResumeCommand emitting --session <id>, supportsResume, extractMetadata capturing the pi session_id, and the persisted resume_of_run_id column via RunsRepo.enqueue) but nothing wires it: dispatchRun (src/runner/dispatch.ts) only calls buildSpawnCommand; the createRun handler (src/server/handlers.ts) and CreateRunBodySchema (src/server/openapi/schemas.ts) ignore resumeOfRunId; and neither client.runs.create (src/lib/client.ts, RunCreateInput) nor the HTTP client (src/lib/http-client.ts, HttpRunCreateInput) forward it. Wire the seam end to end: handler reads resumeOfRunId and OpenAPI documents it; clients forward it (HTTP/wire/in-process parity); dispatchRun validates eligibility (runtime supportsResume; prior run exists, succeeded, same burrow, same agent) and routes to buildResumeCommand(priorRun), failing fast with a clear reason otherwise. Implement ourselves end-to-end; credit the issue author's analysis in the close comment. Tracks https://github.com/jayminwest/burrow/issues/21

title: burrow SQLite DB grows unbounded: no auto_vacuum/VACUUM + burrows rows never pruned
description:
Production warren-deployed box: /data/burrow/db.sqlite reached 1.83 GB while holding ~1 MB of live data (page_count 447,735, freelist 447,496 = 99.95% free pages; runs=1, events=111, burrows=502). Two leaks compound:

1. No space reclamation. src/db/client.ts open pragmas set WAL/synchronous=NORMAL/foreign_keys/busy_timeout=5000 but NO auto_vacuum and nothing ever runs VACUUM. burrow destroy (src/events/archive.ts) archives events/messages/runs to NDJSON then DELETEs the live rows, so every reaped run frees pages that are never returned to the OS. Freelist grows without bound.
2. burrows rows are never deleted - 502 accumulated since mid-May (~one per run).

Impact: operating on the bloated single-threaded bun:sqlite file makes writes/checkpoints/deletes slow enough to stall burrow's event loop. Under concurrent run dispatch this caused warren socket probes to time out ('burrow unreachable: operation timed out'), a run's rows to be deleted mid-flight (run not found at finalize in run-loop.ts), and the run's sandbox to leak.

Fix:
- Add PRAGMA auto_vacuum=INCREMENTAL to open pragmas in src/db/client.ts + periodic PRAGMA incremental_vacuum (or a maintenance VACUUM job).
- Prune burrows rows on destroy (or a retention sweep) so they stop accumulating.
- Make destroy not race an in-flight run / live stream consumer (fixes 'run not found at finalize' + leaked workspace).

SELF-HEALING STARTUP VACUUM (clears existing bloated installs automatically on deploy):
GOTCHA: PRAGMA auto_vacuum=INCREMENTAL does NOT shrink an existing DB on its own - SQLite only applies an auto_vacuum mode change by rebuilding the file, i.e. it requires a one-time full VACUUM to convert a pre-existing database. So just adding the pragma leaves the 1.83 GB file as-is.
Therefore, on startup (in src/db/client.ts open path or a migration), detect a high freelist ratio (e.g. freelist_count / page_count > 0.5) and run VACUUM once, THEN enable auto_vacuum=INCREMENTAL going forward. The VACUUM runs while burrow owns the DB connection (no external-connection lock contention), converts the DB so it never re-bloats, and the deploy clears this box (and any other long-lived install) with no manual ops. The file is 99.95% free pages, so the rebuild copies ~1 MB and finishes in seconds: 1.83 GB -> ~1-5 MB. /data has 41 GB free, so VACUUM scratch space is not a concern.
This means no manual VACUUM is needed - shipping this seed clears the currently overflowing DB on next boot.

title: burrow destroy races in-flight runs: 'run not found at finalize' + leaked sandbox
description: Split off from burrow-05cb (storage-reclamation half is fixed). destroyBurrowStorage prunes runs/events/messages rows for a burrow, but destroyBurrowFully only marks the burrow stopped in the DB (client.burrows.stop) without draining the in-process RunLoop queue for that burrow. Under concurrent dispatch a run can be mid-flight when pruneLiveRows deletes its row, so RunLoop.execute -> runs.finalize hits 'run not found' and the run's sandbox/workspace leaks. Fix: coordinate destroy with the RunLoop so destroy waits for (or aborts) in-flight runs on that burrow before pruning, and make finalize tolerant of a vanished run row.
